stem: Let \( f(x) = [[A]]x^{3} + [[B]]x + ([[C]]) \). Since \( [[A]]>0 \) and \( [[B]]>0 \), \( f'(x) = 3([[A]])x^{2}+[[B]] \) is always positive, so \( f \) is continuous and strictly increasing on all of \( \mathbb{R} \). Direct computation gives \( f([[p]]) = [[fp]] \) and \( f([[q]]) = [[fq]] \), which have opposite signs, so the Intermediate Value Theorem guarantees a root \( c \in ([[p]], [[q]]) \) with \( f(c) = 0 \). Which of the following is \( c \)?
solution: By construction \( [[C]] = -[[A]]([[r]])^{3}-[[B]]([[r]]) \), so \( f([[r]]) = [[A]]([[r]])^{3}+[[B]]([[r]])+([[C]]) = 0 \). Since \( f \) is strictly increasing, this is the ONLY real root, and \( [[p]] < [[r]] < [[q]] \), so \( c = [[r]] = [[answer]] \) is the root the IVT guarantees.
distractor_rationales: This reports the left endpoint of the bracketing interval, not the actual root.
This reports the right endpoint of the bracketing interval, not the actual root.
This is the root shifted one unit too high.
This is the root shifted one unit too low.
This is off by two from the correct root.
This is off by two from the correct root.

stem: Define \( g(x) = \dfrac{[[m_num]]}{[[m_den]]}x + ([[k]]) \). Since \( \left|\dfrac{[[m_num]]}{[[m_den]]}\right| < 1 \), \( g \) is continuous and maps some closed bounded interval into itself, so the fixed-point corollary of the IVT guarantees a point \( c \) with \( g(c) = c \). What is \( c \)?
solution: Setting \( g(c)=c \) gives \( \dfrac{[[m_num]]}{[[m_den]]}c + ([[k]]) = c \), so \( c\left(1-\dfrac{[[m_num]]}{[[m_den]]}\right) = [[k]] \), which gives \( c = \dfrac{[[k]]}{1-\frac{[[m_num]]}{[[m_den]]}} = [[answer]] \).
distractor_rationales: This has the wrong sign on the slope term in the denominator.
This multiplies by \( 1-m \) instead of dividing by it.
This has the wrong overall sign on the correct expression.
This divides \( k \) by \( m \) instead of by \( 1-m \).
This is off by two from the correct fixed point.
This is off by two from the correct fixed point.

stem: Let \( f(x) = [[A]]\sqrt{x} + ([[B]]) \), which is continuous and strictly increasing on \( [[[p]], [[q]]] \subset [0,\infty) \). Since \( f([[p]]) < [[T]] < f([[q]]) \), the Intermediate Value Theorem guarantees a point \( c \in ([[p]], [[q]]) \) with \( f(c) = [[T]] \). What is \( c \)?
solution: Setting \( f(c) = [[T]] \) gives \( [[A]]\sqrt{c} = [[T]]-([[B]]) \), so \( \sqrt{c} = \dfrac{[[T]]-([[B]])}{[[A]]} \), and squaring gives \( c = \left(\dfrac{[[T]]-([[B]])}{[[A]]}\right)^{2} = [[answer]] \).
distractor_rationales: This has the wrong sign on \( B \) inside the ratio before squaring.
This forgets to square the ratio after isolating \( \sqrt{c} \).
This is the correct squared ratio shifted one unit too high.
This squares only the numerator, forgetting to divide \( A \) before squaring.
This is off by two from the correct value of \( c \).
This is off by two from the correct value of \( c \).
